title: Structured vs Unstructured Data
concept: Structured data sits neatly in rows and columns; unstructured data is everything else — text, images, audio — messy, huge, and where modern AI shines.

exercises.prompt: Recap: "red" and "Tokyo" are what kind of data?
exercises.explain: Right — they're labels/groups, so categorical data.

cards.title: Two big buckets
cards.body: Some data fits neatly in a table; most of it doesn't. That split shapes how computers and AI handle it.

cards.title: Structured data
cards.body: Organized in rows and columns, like a spreadsheet or database: names, ages, prices. Easy to search, sort, and total.

cards.title: Unstructured data
cards.body: No neat table: emails, photos, voice notes, videos, social posts. Roughly 80–90% of the world's data is this kind.

cards.title: Where each shows up
cards.body:
Structured: bank transactions, inventory lists, sign-up forms.
Unstructured: your camera roll, WhatsApp voice notes, YouTube, customer reviews.

cards.title: Messy data used to be useless
cards.body: Old software loved tidy tables and choked on images and audio. Deep learning finally unlocked unstructured data — a big reason AI feels magical now.

cards.title: The split
cards.body: How to tell them apart at a glance:

exercises.prompt: A spreadsheet of customer names, ages, and cities is…
exercises.explain: Rows and columns = structured data — easy to search and sort.

exercises.prompt: Your phone's camera roll (thousands of photos) is…
exercises.explain: Photos don't fit a tidy table — that's unstructured data.

exercises.prompt: True or false: most of the world's data is unstructured.
exercises.explain: True — the vast majority (photos, video, text, audio) is unstructured.

exercises.sentence: Data neatly organized in rows and columns is called ___ data.

exercises.prompt: Why does modern AI handle messy things like images and voice so much better than older software?

options.text: Deep learning finally made unstructured data usable
It doesn't — it's exactly the same

exercises.explain: Exactly — deep learning cracked unstructured data (images, text, audio), which older rule-based software couldn't handle.
